1:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh: for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him:" The witnesses note God's purpose in hardening Pharaoh's heart. On "I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants," the grammatical voices note the hardening. Calvin and Henry note that "I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him" — God declared he had hardened Pharaoh's heart (and his servants'), in the judicial hardening of the obstinate king (the hardening foretold, Exodus 4:21; 7:3; following Pharaoh's own self-hardening, Exodus 8:15, 32); the divine sovereignty over the heart of the proud king.

On "that I might shew these my signs before him," interpreters expound the purpose. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the purpose — "that I might shew these my signs before him": God hardened Pharaoh that he might display his signs and wonders (the plagues, the deliverance); the purpose of the hardening (the multiplying of the signs, Exodus 7:3-4; the showing of God's power, Exodus 9:16; the glory of God in the judgments); the providence overruling the king's obstinacy for God's own ends (the manifestation of his power and the instruction of his people); the sovereign purpose (Romans 9:17-18, "that I might shew my power in thee... whom he will he hardeneth").

On the hardening, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: God's hardening (the judicial giving-up of the obstinate to their own obstinacy, after long resistance to mercy and warning) — the purpose (the signs displayed, the power shown, the people instructed, vv.2); the providence overruling the hardened heart. The verse displays God's purpose in hardening Pharaoh's heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hardening — "I have hardened his heart... that I might shew these my signs," the purpose (Exodus 4:21, Calvin, Henry); "I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants," the divine sovereignty (Exodus 9:16, Calvin); the providence (Romans 9:17, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardening God. The verse marks the hardening: of Pharaoh. The verse displays the purpose: to show the signs. The verse displays the power: to be shown. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The hardening displays God's sovereign purpose in the signs. The verse displays the LORD: hardening. The verse displays the Pharaoh: obstinate. The verse displays the signs: to be shown. God's purpose in hardening Pharaoh's heart displays the sovereign design: I have hardened his heart, that I might shew my signs (Exodus 4:21; 9:16; Romans 9:17-18) — the obstinacy overruled for the displaying of God's power and the instruction of his people. The verse displays the signs told to generations (v.2).
2:
"And that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt, and my signs which I have done among them; that ye may know how that I am the LORD." The witnesses note the signs to be told to generations — the crux of the catechesis. On "that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son," interpreters expound the purpose. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great word — "that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt": a further purpose of the plagues — that the mighty works of God in Egypt might be told to the children and the children's children, from generation to generation; the catechetical purpose (the parents rehearsing God's wonders to their children, the foundation of the Passover instruction, Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8, 14; Deuteronomy 6:20-21; Psalm 78:4-7, "we will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD"); the handing-down of the knowledge of God's redemptive acts; the works of God recounted that faith might be passed on.

On "what things I have wrought in Egypt, and my signs," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the works. Clarke and Ellicott note the "things wrought" and "signs" (the plagues, the deliverance) — the great redemptive acts to be remembered and told; the matter of the children's instruction; the wonders of God recounted.

On "that ye may know how that I am the LORD," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the knowledge: "that ye may know how that I am the LORD" — the end of the telling: that Israel (and their generations) might know the LORD (his power, faithfulness, and covenant); the knowledge of God passed on through the rehearsing of his works; the purpose of the signs (the knowledge of the LORD, Exodus 6:7; 7:5). The verse displays the signs to be told to generations.

The witnesses agree the substance: the plagues were wrought that they might be told to the children's children, that all generations might know the LORD (Calvin, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the catechesis: "that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son... what things I have wrought" — the catechetical purpose, the parents rehearsing God's redemptive acts to their children from generation to generation (Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8; Deuteronomy 6:20-21; Psalm 78:4-7), the handing-down of the knowledge of God; "that ye may know how that I am the LORD" — the knowledge of God passed on through the telling of his works. The verse displays the catechesis crux. The verse marks the telling: to son and son's son. The verse displays the works: wrought in Egypt. The verse displays the knowledge: I am the LORD. The verse displays the generations: instructed. The telling displays the works of God handed down to the children. The verse displays the parents: rehearsing. The verse displays the children: instructed. The verse displays the LORD: known. The signs to be told to generations display the crux: tell in the ears of thy son what I have wrought, that ye may know I am the LORD (Exodus 12:26-27; 13:8; Psalm 78:4-7) — the catechetical purpose, the redemptive acts handed down, the knowledge of God passed on through the generations. The verse displays the demand to humble himself (v.3).
3:
"And Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me." The witnesses note God's reproof of Pharaoh's pride. On "How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me?" the grammatical voices note the reproof. Calvin and Gill note that "Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me?" — God reproved Pharaoh for his persistent pride and refusal to humble himself before the LORD; the rebuke of the obstinate king (who would not bow despite the plagues); the call to humility (the very thing the proud king refused); the divine remonstrance.

On "refuse to humble thyself before me," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the pride. Calvin and Wesley note the refusal to humble himself (the root sin of Pharaoh, the pride that would not submit to God) — the obstinacy; the call to repentance refused; the proud heart unbroken by the judgments.

On "let my people go, that they may serve me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the demand: "let my people go, that they may serve me" — the renewed demand (Exodus 5:1; 8:1, 20); the release pressed (before the plague of locusts); the worship of God the end; the demand joined to the reproof. The verse displays God's reproof of Pharaoh's pride.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reproof — "How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go," the rebuke (Calvin, Gill); "refuse to humble thyself before me," the pride (Calvin, Wesley); "let my people go, that they may serve me," the demand (Exodus 8:1, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reproving LORD. The verse marks the reproof: how long. The verse displays the pride: refusing to humble. The verse displays the demand: let my people go. The verse displays the worship: to serve me. The reproof displays God rebuking the proud king. The verse displays the LORD: reproving. The verse displays the Pharaoh: proud. The verse displays the demand: renewed. God's reproof of Pharaoh's pride displays the rebuke of the obstinate: how long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself? let my people go (Exodus 8:1) — the call to the humility the proud king refused, the demand pressed. The verse displays the threat of locusts (vv.4-6).
4:
"Else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast:" The witnesses note the threat of the plague of locusts. On "if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts," the grammatical voices note the threat. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast" — God threatened, if Pharaoh refused, to bring the locusts upon Egypt on the morrow; the eighth plague announced (the locusts devouring all the land, vv.5-6); the warning before the judgment; the plague conditioned on the refusal.

On "to morrow will I bring the locusts," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the locusts. Calvin and Ellicott note the locusts (the devouring swarms, a dreaded plague in the East, Joel 1:4; 2:25) — the eighth plague; the judgment that would consume all that the hail had left (v.5); the dreaded scourge.

On "into thy coast," the grammatical-historical note the extent: "into thy coast (border, territory)" — the locusts upon all Egypt; the all-devouring plague; the judgment (the land stripped bare, v.15); the providence (the plague following the refusal). The verse displays the threat of the plague of locusts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the threat — "if thou refuse... to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast," the plague threatened (vv.5-6, Keil, Gill); "to morrow will I bring the locusts," the locusts (Joel 1:4, Calvin, Ellicott); "into thy coast," the extent (v.15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the threatening LORD. The verse marks the condition: if thou refuse. The verse displays the locusts: tomorrow. The verse displays the coast: all Egypt. The verse displays the judgment: announced. The threat displays God warning the plague of locusts. The verse displays the LORD: threatening. The verse displays the locusts: to come. The verse displays the Egypt: to be stripped. The threat of the plague of locusts displays the warning before judgment: if thou refuse, tomorrow will I bring the locusts (vv.5-6; Joel 1:4) — the eighth plague announced, the all-devouring scourge. The verse displays the locusts covering the land (v.5).
5:
"And they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field:" The very brief verse notes the locusts devouring all that the hail had left. The grammatical voices note the devouring. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they shall cover the face of the earth... and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail" — the locusts would cover the whole face of the earth (so thick none could see the ground) and devour all that the hail (the seventh plague, Exodus 9:25) had left; the all-devouring plague (the completing of the destruction begun by the hail); the judgment stripping the land bare.

On "cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth," the witnesses note the multitude. Gill notes the locusts covering the ground (so thick the earth could not be seen) — the vast swarms; the all-covering plague.

On "eat the residue... which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree," the grammatical-historical note the destruction: "eat the residue... and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field" — the locusts devouring all the remaining crops and trees (what the hail had spared); the complete stripping of the land; the judgment leaving nothing green (v.15); the providence (the plague that would force Pharaoh's relenting, v.16). The verse displays the locusts devouring all.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the devouring — "they shall cover the face of the earth... and they shall eat the residue... from the hail," the all-devouring (Exodus 9:25, Keil, Gill); "cover the face of the earth," the multitude (Gill); "eat the residue... and shall eat every tree," the destruction (v.15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the devouring locusts. The verse marks the locusts: covering the earth. The verse displays the residue: of the hail. The verse displays the trees: eaten. The verse displays the land: stripped. The devouring displays the locusts consuming all. The verse displays the locusts: covering. The verse displays the crops: devoured. The verse displays the Egypt: stripped bare. The locusts devouring all display the completing judgment: they shall cover the earth and eat the residue of the hail, every tree (Exodus 9:25) — the all-devouring plague stripping the land bare. The verse displays the houses filled (v.6).
6:
"And they shall fill thy houses, and the houses of all thy servants, and the houses of all the Egyptians; which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen, since the day that they were upon the earth unto this day. And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh." The very brief verse notes the unprecedented plague and Moses' departure. The grammatical voices note the magnitude. Ellicott and Gill note that "they shall fill thy houses... which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen... And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh" — the locusts would fill even the houses (not only the fields); a plague unprecedented (greater than any the Egyptians' fathers had seen, vv.14); and Moses, having delivered the warning, turned and went out from Pharaoh; the magnitude of the judgment; the departure of the prophet.

On "which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen," the witnesses note the unprecedented. Gill notes the plague unlike any before (greater than all former locust-plagues, v.14) — the extraordinary judgment; the unprecedented scourge.

On "he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh," the grammatical-historical note the departure: "he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh" — Moses leaving abruptly (the warning given, the message complete); the prophet's departure (perhaps in indignation at the king's obstinacy); the providence (the warning delivered, the plague to follow, vv.13-15). The verse displays the unprecedented plague and Moses' departure.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the magnitude — "they shall fill thy houses... which neither thy fathers... have seen... And he... went out from Pharaoh," the unprecedented plague (v.14, Ellicott, Gill); "which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen," the unprecedented (Gill); "he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh," the departure (vv.13-15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning Moses. The verse marks the houses: filled. The verse displays the plague: unprecedented. The verse displays the Moses: departing. The verse displays the judgment: to follow. The warning displays the magnitude of the plague and Moses' departure. The verse displays the locusts: to fill the houses. The verse displays the plague: unequalled. The verse displays the Moses: gone out. The unprecedented plague and Moses' departure display the magnitude of the judgment: a plague their fathers never saw; and Moses went out (v.14) — the unequalled scourge announced, the prophet departing. The verse displays the servants' appeal (v.7).
7:
"And Pharaoh's servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go, that they may serve the LORD their God: knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?" The witnesses note Pharaoh's servants pleading for Israel's release. On "How long shall this man be a snare unto us?" the grammatical voices note the appeal. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh's servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go" — Pharaoh's own servants (the courtiers, lately hardened, Exodus 10:1) now pleaded with him to let Israel go, calling Moses "a snare" (a trap, the cause of Egypt's ruin); the appeal of the servants (the plagues having broken even their obstinacy); the pressure on the king from his own court.

On "let the men go, that they may serve the LORD their God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the counsel. Calvin and Clarke note the servants' counsel (let Israel go) — the courtiers yielding before the king; the pressure of the ruined land; the appeal to reason.

On "knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?" the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the ruin: "knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?" — the servants' remonstrance (Egypt already ruined by the plagues, the hail, Exodus 9:25, and worse to come); the obstinacy of the king exceeding even his hardened courtiers (they yielding, he not); the providence (the king's obstinacy isolating him even from his own). The verse displays Pharaoh's servants pleading for Israel's release.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appeal — "How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go... knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?" the servants' plea (Exodus 10:1, Ellicott, Gill); "let the men go, that they may serve the LORD," the counsel (Calvin, Clarke); "knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?" the ruin (Exodus 9:25, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading servants. The verse marks the appeal: how long. The verse displays the snare: Moses. The verse displays the counsel: let them go. The verse displays the ruin: Egypt destroyed. The appeal displays the servants pressing the king. The verse displays the servants: pleading. The verse displays the Pharaoh: pressed. The verse displays the Egypt: ruined. Pharaoh's servants pleading for Israel's release display the king isolated in his obstinacy: how long shall this man be a snare? let them go; Egypt is destroyed (Exodus 9:25) — even his courtiers yielding while the king hardened on. The verse displays the partial concession (vv.8-11).
8:
"And Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?" The very brief verse notes Pharaoh recalling Moses to negotiate. The grammatical voices note the recall. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?" — Pharaoh, pressed by his servants (v.7), recalled Moses and Aaron and seemed to yield ("Go, serve the LORD"), but added the question "who are they that shall go?" — beginning to negotiate, to limit the concession; the seeming yielding (with a catch); the bargaining of the king.

On "Go, serve the LORD your God," the witnesses note the concession. Gill notes Pharaoh's apparent yielding ("Go, serve the LORD") — the seeming concession; the relenting under pressure (yet to be qualified, v.10-11).

On "but who are they that shall go?" the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the catch: "but who are they that shall go?" — Pharaoh's qualifying question (seeking to limit who should go); the bargaining (the prelude to the compromise, vv.10-11, the men only); the half-yielding (the concession with a condition); the providence (the king still resisting the full release). The verse displays Pharaoh recalling Moses to negotiate.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the recall — "Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?" the negotiation (v.7, Keil, Gill); "Go, serve the LORD your God," the concession (Gill); "but who are they that shall go?" the catch (vv.10-11, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the negotiating Pharaoh. The verse marks the recall: Moses brought back. The verse displays the concession: go, serve. The verse displays the catch: who shall go. The verse displays the compromise: forming. The recall displays Pharaoh bargaining over the release. The verse displays the Pharaoh: negotiating. The verse displays the concession: qualified. The verse displays the compromise: coming. Pharaoh recalling Moses to negotiate displays the half-yielding with a catch: go, serve the LORD; but who shall go? (vv.10-11) — the seeming concession qualified, the prelude to the compromise. The verse displays Moses' full demand (v.9).
9:
"And Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast unto the LORD." The witnesses note Moses demanding the whole nation go. On "We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters," the grammatical voices note the demand. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go" — Moses demanded that the whole nation go: young and old, sons and daughters, flocks and herds — none left behind; the full demand (the complete release, no compromise); the whole people and their substance.

On "with our flocks and with our herds will we go," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the completeness. Calvin and Gill note that even the flocks and herds must go (for the sacrifices, and as their property) — the complete release; no part left in Egypt; the full separation.

On "for we must hold a feast unto the LORD," the grammatical-historical note the worship: "for we must hold a feast unto the LORD" — the purpose (the feast, the worship of God, requiring all the people and their offerings); the demand grounded on God's claim; the full obedience (the whole nation set apart to serve God, no compromise). The verse displays Moses demanding the whole nation go.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the demand — "We will go with our young and with our old... with our flocks and with our herds will we go," the full demand (Keil, Ellicott); "with our flocks and with our herds will we go," the completeness (Calvin, Gill); "for we must hold a feast unto the LORD," the worship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the demanding Moses. The verse marks the demand: all go. The verse displays the young and old: included. The verse displays the flocks: with them. The verse displays the worship: the feast. The demand displays Moses requiring the whole nation. The verse displays the Moses: demanding. The verse displays the people: all. The verse displays the separation: full. Moses demanding the whole nation go displays the full release: we will go with young and old, flocks and herds, to hold a feast unto the LORD — the complete separation, no part left behind, the full obedience. The verse displays Pharaoh's compromise (vv.10-11).
10:
"And he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you." The witnesses note Pharaoh's mocking refusal of the full release. On "Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones," the grammatical voices note the mockery. Ellicott and Gill note that "he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones" — Pharaoh mockingly refused: he would as soon let them go with their little ones as the LORD would be with them (i.e., never); the sneering refusal (the proud king mocking both the demand and the LORD); the scornful rejection of the full release.

On "Let the LORD be so with you," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the scorn. Calvin and Wesley note Pharaoh's scornful words (mocking the LORD and the demand) — the contempt; the obstinacy; the king's pride sneering at God.

On "look to it; for evil is before you," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the threat: "look to it; for evil is before you" — Pharaoh's threat (some evil, mischief, or danger awaited them if they pressed the full demand; perhaps a charge that they meant rebellion or flight); the menace; the providence (the king's mockery and threat answered by the plague, vv.12-15). The verse displays Pharaoh's mocking refusal of the full release.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mockery — "Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones... evil is before you," the scornful refusal (Ellicott, Gill); "Let the LORD be so with you," the scorn (Calvin, Wesley); "look to it; for evil is before you," the threat (vv.12-15, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mocking Pharaoh. The verse marks the mockery: the LORD be so with you. The verse displays the refusal: of the little ones. The verse displays the threat: evil before you. The verse displays the scorn: of the king. The mockery displays Pharaoh scorning the full demand. The verse displays the Pharaoh: mocking. The verse displays the LORD: derided. The verse displays the threat: menacing. Pharaoh's mocking refusal of the full release displays the proud scorn: let the LORD be so with you, as I will let your little ones go; evil is before you (vv.12-15) — the sneering refusal of the full demand, answered by the plague. The verse displays the compromise of the men only (v.11).
11:
"Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire. And they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence." The witnesses note Pharaoh's third compromise — the men only — the crux of the half-release. On "go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD," interpreters expound the compromise. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the third compromise — "Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire": Pharaoh offered a third partial concession — let the men go to worship, but not the little ones (the women and children, and the cattle, to be kept in Egypt, as hostages, that the men would return); the half-release (the men only, the families held back); the witnesses note the lesson — another of the world's (and Satan's) compromises: let the grown serve God, but keep back the children (or, keep something back as a pledge of return to the world; serve God yourself, but leave your family in Egypt); the snare of the partial release (keeping the children, the future, in bondage; the families not wholly given to God); the partial obedience God's people must refuse (the whole household to be set apart, the children with the parents in the service of God, Exodus 10:9; the covenant including the children, Acts 2:39).

On "for that ye did desire," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the twist. Gill notes Pharaoh's twisting (as if the men's worship were all they had asked) — the misrepresentation; the grudging concession.

On "they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the wrath: "they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence" — Moses and Aaron driven out in anger (the king's wrath at the refusal of his compromise); the rupture; the providence (the king's obstinacy bringing the plague, vv.12-15). The verse displays Pharaoh's third compromise — the men only.

The witnesses agree the substance: Pharaoh offered to let only the men go, keeping the children, and drove Moses out (Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the half-release: "go now ye that are men... but not the little ones" — the third of Pharaoh's compromises (let the men serve God, keep the children in Egypt); another of the world's snares: serve God yourself, but leave your family (your children, your future) in bondage to the world; the partial release God's people must refuse, the whole household to be given to God (Exodus 10:9; the children of the covenant, Acts 2:39); the families not to be kept as a pledge of return to Egypt. The verse displays the third-compromise crux. The verse marks the compromise: the men only. The verse displays the children: kept back. The verse displays the snare: the families held. The verse displays the refusal: of Pharaoh's terms. The compromise displays Pharaoh's half-release. The verse displays the Pharaoh: compromising. The verse displays the children: in bondage. The verse displays the household: divided. Pharaoh's third compromise displays the crux: go ye that are men, but keep the little ones (Exodus 10:9; Acts 2:39) — the half-release, serve God but leave your children in Egypt, the world's snare God's people must refuse, the whole household given to God. The verse displays the plague of locusts (vv.12-15).
12:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up upon the land of Egypt, and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left." The witnesses note the command for the plague of locusts. On "Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts," the grammatical voices note the command. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up... and eat every herb of the land" — God commanded Moses to stretch his hand over Egypt to bring the locusts, that they might devour every herb and all that the hail had left; the eighth plague executed (after the rupture, v.11); the judgment on the obstinate king; the all-devouring scourge.

On "that they may come up upon the land of Egypt," the witnesses note the locusts. Gill notes the locusts coming up (brought by God's hand, by the east wind, v.13) — the eighth plague; the devouring swarms.

On "eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the destruction: "eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left" — the locusts completing the destruction begun by the hail (Exodus 9:25); the land stripped bare; the judgment (nothing green left, v.15); the providence (the plague forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16). The verse displays the command for the plague of locusts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Stretch out thine hand... for the locusts... and eat every herb... all that the hail hath left," the plague executed (v.11, Henry, Gill); "that they may come up upon the land of Egypt," the locusts (v.13, Gill); "eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left," the destruction (Exodus 9:25, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding LORD. The verse marks the command: stretch out thy hand. The verse displays the locusts: to come up. The verse displays the herbs: devoured. The verse displays the plague: executed. The command displays God bringing the plague of locusts. The verse displays the Moses: charged. The verse displays the locusts: to come. The verse displays the land: stripped. The command for the plague of locusts displays the judgment executed: stretch out thy hand for the locusts, to eat all the hail had left (Exodus 9:25) — the eighth plague brought, the land stripped bare. The verse displays the east wind (v.13).
13:
"And Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the LORD brought an east wind upon the land all that day, and all that night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts." The very brief verse notes the east wind bringing the locusts. The grammatical voices note the means. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the LORD brought an east wind upon the land all that day, and all that night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts" — Moses stretched out his rod, and the LORD brought an east wind (blowing a day and a night) which brought the locusts in the morning; the means of the plague (the east wind, the natural agent in God's hand, bringing the swarms from the east, Arabia or beyond); the providence using natural means; the plague come.

On "the LORD brought an east wind," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the agent. Wesley and Clarke note the east wind (the natural means God used to bring the locusts) — the providence (God ruling the winds to bring the plague, as he would the wind to remove them, v.19); the natural agent of the supernatural judgment.

On "the east wind brought the locusts," the grammatical-historical note the swarm: "the east wind brought the locusts" — the locusts borne in by the wind (the vast swarms); the eighth plague come; the judgment (the land to be devoured, vv.14-15); the providence (the timing, the means, all of God). The verse displays the east wind bringing the locusts.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the means — "the LORD brought an east wind... and the east wind brought the locusts," the agent (Keil, Gill); "the LORD brought an east wind," the providence (v.19, Wesley, Clarke); "the east wind brought the locusts," the swarm (vv.14-15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wind-borne locusts. The verse marks the rod: stretched. The verse displays the wind: east. The verse displays the locusts: brought. The verse displays the providence: ruling the wind. The means displays the east wind bringing the plague. The verse displays the LORD: bringing the wind. The verse displays the locusts: borne in. The verse displays the judgment: come. The east wind bringing the locusts displays the providence using natural means: the LORD brought an east wind, and it brought the locusts (v.19) — the natural agent of the supernatural judgment, all of God. The verse displays the locusts covering Egypt (v.14).
14:
"And the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such." The very brief verse notes the unequalled swarm of locusts. The grammatical voices note the magnitude. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such" — the locusts covered all Egypt, settling in every part; a swarm "very grievous" (severe), unequalled before or after (the greatest locust-plague ever, v.6); the magnitude of the judgment; the extraordinary scourge.

On "very grievous were they," the witnesses note the severity. Gill notes the "very grievous" (heavy, severe) swarm — the dreadful plague; the all-covering locusts.

On "before them there were no such locusts... neither after them shall be such," the grammatical-historical note the unequalled: "before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such" — the plague unequalled (the greatest in history, the like never before or after in Egypt); the extraordinary judgment; the providence (the unequalled plague forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16). The verse displays the unequalled swarm of locusts.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the magnitude — "the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt... very grievous... no such locusts," the unequalled swarm (v.6, Keil, Ellicott); "very grievous were they," the severity (Gill); "before them there were no such locusts... neither after them shall be such," the unequalled (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the swarming locusts. The verse marks the locusts: over all Egypt. The verse displays the swarm: very grievous. The verse displays the plague: unequalled. The verse displays the judgment: extraordinary. The magnitude displays the unequalled locust-plague. The verse displays the locusts: covering Egypt. The verse displays the swarm: unequalled. The verse displays the land: to be devoured. The unequalled swarm of locusts displays the extraordinary judgment: very grievous, no such locusts before or after (v.6) — the greatest plague of its kind, covering all Egypt. The verse displays the land darkened and devoured (v.15).
15:
"For they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened; and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left: and there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt." The very brief verse notes the land stripped bare by the locusts. The grammatical voices note the destruction. Ellicott and Gill note that "they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened; and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left: and there remained not any green thing" — the locusts covered the ground (darkening the land by their multitude) and devoured every herb and all the fruit the hail had left, leaving not a green thing in all Egypt; the complete destruction (the land stripped utterly bare); the judgment fulfilled (the famine of the field).

On "the land was darkened," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the multitude. Wesley and Clarke note the land "darkened" by the swarms (so thick they hid the ground, a foretaste of the darkness to come, v.21) — the vast multitude; the all-covering plague.

On "there remained not any green thing," the grammatical-historical note the desolation: "there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field" — the complete stripping of the land (nothing green left, total desolation); the judgment fulfilled; the providence (the ruin forcing Pharaoh's relenting, v.16). The verse displays the land stripped bare by the locusts.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the destruction — "they covered the face of the whole earth... and did eat every herb... there remained not any green thing," the desolation (Ellicott, Gill); "the land was darkened," the multitude (v.21, Wesley, Clarke); "there remained not any green thing," the desolation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the stripped land. The verse marks the locusts: covering the earth. The verse displays the land: darkened. The verse displays the green: all gone. The verse displays the desolation: complete. The destruction displays the land stripped bare. The verse displays the locusts: devouring. The verse displays the Egypt: desolate. The verse displays the judgment: fulfilled. The land stripped bare by the locusts displays the complete desolation: they covered and darkened the land, leaving not a green thing (v.21) — the utter stripping of Egypt, the judgment fulfilled. The verse displays Pharaoh's hasty confession (v.16).
16:
"Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you." The witnesses note Pharaoh's hasty confession of sin. On "Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste," the grammatical voices note the alarm. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you" — Pharaoh, alarmed by the ruin (the land stripped bare, v.15), hastily called Moses and Aaron and confessed "I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you"; the confession under pressure (the proud king brought to own his sin); the haste (the urgency of the alarm).

On "I have sinned against the LORD your God," interpreters weigh the confession. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Pharaoh's words — "I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you": Pharaoh confessed sin (against God and against Moses and Aaron); yet the witnesses weigh it as a false or partial repentance — the confession of fear and necessity (wrung out by the plague), not of a broken, contrite heart; the confession that would vanish when the plague was removed (v.20); the lesson — the forced confession of the afflicted (as Saul, 1 Samuel 15:24, 30; Judas, Matthew 27:4) that is not true repentance; the words of confession without the change of heart; the repentance of the damned (owning sin under the lash, yet hardening again when relieved).

On "and against you," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the partiality: "and against you" — Pharaoh owning his wrong to Moses and Aaron (yet the confession of necessity); the providence (the confession that would not hold, the king relenting under pressure, hardening when relieved, v.20). The verse displays Pharaoh's hasty confession of sin.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the confession — "I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you," the confession under pressure (v.15, Keil, Gill); "I have sinned against the LORD your God," the false repentance (1 Samuel 15:24, Calvin); "and against you," the partiality (v.20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confessing Pharaoh. The verse marks the confession: I have sinned. The verse displays the haste: alarmed. The verse displays the repentance: false. The verse displays the relenting: under pressure. The confession displays Pharaoh's forced, false repentance. The verse displays the Pharaoh: confessing. The verse displays the sin: owned. The verse displays the heart: unchanged. Pharaoh's hasty confession of sin displays the forced, false repentance: I have sinned against the LORD and against you (v.15; 1 Samuel 15:24) — the confession of fear and necessity, not of a contrite heart, soon to vanish (v.20). The verse displays his plea for forgiveness (v.17).
17:
"Now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the LORD your God, that he may take away from me this death only." The witnesses note Pharaoh's plea for forgiveness and removal. On "forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once," the grammatical voices note the plea. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the LORD your God, that he may take away from me this death only" — Pharaoh begged forgiveness ("this once") and asked Moses to entreat the LORD to remove the plague (the locusts, "this death"); the plea of necessity (the king begging relief, not truly repenting); the appeal to Moses' intercession.

On "my sin only this once," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the false repentance. Calvin and Wesley note "only this once" (the king's words betraying the falseness — seeking relief from this plague, not a changed heart) — the repentance of necessity; the plea that would not hold (the same false relenting as before, v.20).

On "that he may take away from me this death only," the grammatical-historical note the relief: "that he may take away from me this death only" — Pharaoh seeking only the removal of the plague ("this death," the locusts threatening famine and death); the relief, not the repentance; the providence (the plague removed, yet the king hardening again, v.20). The verse displays Pharaoh's plea for forgiveness and removal.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plea — "forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once... that he may take away from me this death only," the appeal (v.16, Keil, Gill); "my sin only this once," the false repentance (v.20, Calvin, Wesley); "that he may take away from me this death only," the relief (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading Pharaoh. The verse marks the plea: forgive this once. The verse displays the intercession: sought. The verse displays the death: to be removed. The verse displays the relief: not repentance. The plea displays Pharaoh seeking relief, not change. The verse displays the Pharaoh: pleading. The verse displays the locusts: to be removed. The verse displays the heart: unchanged. Pharaoh's plea for forgiveness and removal displays the relief sought, not repentance: forgive this once, take away this death (v.16) — the king begging relief from the plague, not a changed heart, soon hardening (v.20). The verse displays Moses' intercession (v.18).
18:
"And he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD." The very brief verse notes Moses interceding for the removal of the locusts. The grammatical voices note the prayer. Gill and Calvin note that "he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD" — Moses, despite the king's false repentance, went out and prayed (entreated) the LORD to remove the locusts; the intercession (the deliverer praying for the very king who had driven him out, v.11); the readiness to forgive and pray (returning good for evil); the dependence on God.

On "he went out from Pharaoh," the witnesses note the departure. Gill notes Moses leaving to pray — the intercessor's resort to God; the going out to entreat.

On "intreated the LORD," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the intercession: "intreated the LORD" — Moses praying for the removal (as before, Exodus 8:12, 30); the intercessor seeking the deliverance; the providence (God answering, the locusts removed, v.19); the prophet's mercy toward the obstinate king. The verse displays Moses interceding for the removal of the locusts.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prayer — "he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD," the intercession (v.17, Gill, Calvin); "he went out from Pharaoh," the departure (Gill); "intreated the LORD," the prayer (Exodus 8:30, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the praying Moses. The verse marks the departure: from Pharaoh. The verse displays the prayer: entreated the LORD. The verse displays the intercession: for the king. The verse displays the mercy: of the prophet. The prayer displays Moses interceding for the obstinate king. The verse displays the Moses: praying. The verse displays the LORD: entreated. The verse displays the locusts: to be removed. Moses interceding for the removal of the locusts displays the prophet's mercy: he went out and intreated the LORD (v.17; Exodus 8:30) — praying for the very king who drove him out, returning good for evil. The verse displays the locusts removed (v.19). That Moses, so lately driven out of Pharaoh's presence with the king's third compromise refused (Exodus 10:11), yet turned at once to pray for the removal of the plague, shows the meekness of the man of God, who rendered intercession for injury and sought the relief of the very land that oppressed his people (Exodus 8:30).
19:
"And the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt." The very brief verse notes the locusts swept away into the Red Sea. The grammatical voices note the removal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt" — God turned a strong west wind which swept the locusts away and cast them into the Red Sea; the complete removal ("not one locust" left); the providence (as the east wind brought them, v.13, the west wind removed them — all of God); the deliverance fulfilled.

On "a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts," the witnesses note the agent. Gill notes the west wind (the natural means God used to remove the locusts, as the east wind brought them) — the providence ruling the winds; the complete sweeping-away.

On "cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust," the grammatical-historical note the completeness: "cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt" — the locusts drowned in the Red Sea (the same sea where Pharaoh's host would perish, Exodus 14:27-28); the complete removal (not one left); the deliverance (proving God's hand); the providence (the mercy that Pharaoh would abuse, v.20). The verse displays the locusts swept away into the Red Sea.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the removal — "the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind... cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust," the deliverance (v.13, Keil, Gill); "a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts," the agent (Gill); "cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust," the completeness (Exodus 14:27, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the removing LORD. The verse marks the wind: west. The verse displays the locusts: swept away. The verse displays the Red Sea: their grave. The verse displays the removal: complete. The removal displays God sweeping away the plague. The verse displays the LORD: turning the wind. The verse displays the locusts: drowned. The verse displays the Egypt: cleared. The locusts swept away into the Red Sea display the complete deliverance: a west wind cast them into the Red sea; not one remained (Exodus 14:27-28) — the providence ruling the winds, the mercy Pharaoh would abuse (v.20). The verse displays Pharaoh's renewed hardening (v.20).
20:
"But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh hardened again after the locusts. The grammatical voices note the hardening. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go" — Pharaoh, the locusts removed (v.19), was hardened again (the LORD hardening, in the judicial giving-up of the obstinate, as Exodus 9:12; 10:1) and refused to let Israel go; the broken confession (the false repentance vanishing when the plague was lifted, vv.16-17); the renewed obstinacy.

On "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the reformed and witnesses note the judgment. Gill notes the LORD hardening (the judicial hardening of the obstinate king, after his own self-hardening and his abuse of the mercies) — the divine giving-up; the obstinacy confirmed (Romans 1:24-28; 9:18).

On "he would not let the children of Israel go," the grammatical-historical note the refusal: "he would not let the children of Israel go" — the broken promise (the confession of v.16 vanished); the hardened refusal; the providence (the obstinacy bringing the next plague, the darkness, vv.21-23). The verse displays Pharaoh hardened again after the locusts.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the hardening — "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go," the renewed obstinacy (vv.16-17, Ellicott, Gill); "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the judgment (Exodus 9:12; Romans 9:18, Gill); "he would not let the children of Israel go," the refusal (vv.21-23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardened Pharaoh. The verse marks the hardening: by the LORD. The verse displays the confession: broken. The verse displays the refusal: renewed. The verse displays the plague: to follow. The hardening displays Pharaoh's renewed obstinacy. The verse displays the LORD: hardening. The verse displays the Pharaoh: refusing. The verse displays the darkness: to come. Pharaoh hardened again after the locusts displays the broken false repentance: the LORD hardened his heart, he would not let them go (vv.16-17; Romans 9:18) — the confession vanishing when the plague was lifted, the obstinacy bringing the darkness. The verse displays the plague of darkness (vv.21-23).
21:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt." The witnesses note the command for the plague of darkness. On "Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt," the grammatical voices note the command. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt" — God commanded Moses to stretch his hand toward heaven to bring darkness over Egypt; the ninth plague (the thick darkness, three days, vv.22-23); the judgment on Egypt (perhaps on the sun-god Ra, the chief deity, now blotted out); the supernatural darkness.

On "darkness over the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the judgment. Calvin and Ellicott note the darkness (the supernatural, three-days' darkness, far beyond any natural gloom) — the judgment on Egypt's idolatry (the sun, moon, worshipped, now darkened); the terror of the darkness.

On "even darkness which may be felt," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the thickness: "even darkness which may be felt" — the darkness so thick and palpable it could, as it were, be felt (a dense, oppressive, terrifying gloom, perhaps with a thick, clammy mist); the supernatural horror; the judgment (a type of the outer darkness, Matthew 8:12; the darkness of God's wrath, the plagues of the end, Revelation 16:10). The verse displays the command for the plague of darkness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness... even darkness which may be felt," the ninth plague (Henry, Gill); "darkness over the land of Egypt," the judgment (Calvin, Ellicott); "even darkness which may be felt," the thickness (Revelation 16:10, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanding LORD. The verse marks the command: stretch toward heaven. The verse displays the darkness: over Egypt. The verse displays the thickness: may be felt. The verse displays the judgment: the ninth plague. The command displays God bringing the plague of darkness. The verse displays the Moses: charged. The verse displays the darkness: to come. The verse displays the Egypt: in gloom. The command for the plague of darkness displays the supernatural judgment: darkness over Egypt, which may be felt (Revelation 16:10) — the thick, palpable gloom, the judgment on Egypt's idols. The verse displays the three days' darkness (vv.22-23).
22:
"And Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days:" The very brief verse notes the three days' thick darkness. The grammatical voices note the darkness. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days" — Moses stretched out his hand, and a thick darkness covered all Egypt for three days; the ninth plague executed (the supernatural, oppressive gloom, three days); the judgment fulfilled; the darkness over the land.

On "a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the gloom. Calvin and Gill note the "thick darkness" (the dense, palpable, supernatural gloom, v.21) — the terror of the plague; the all-covering darkness.

On "three days," the grammatical-historical note the duration: "three days" — the darkness lasting three full days (the prolonged, oppressive judgment); the terror (the Egyptians shut up in darkness, vv.23); the providence (the judgment on Egypt's idols, the contrast with Israel's light, v.23). The verse displays the three days' thick darkness.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the darkness — "Moses stretched forth his hand... and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days," the plague (v.21, Keil, Gill); "a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt," the gloom (Calvin, Gill); "three days," the duration (v.23, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the darkened Egypt. The verse marks the hand: stretched. The verse displays the darkness: thick. The verse displays the days: three. The verse displays the plague: executed. The darkness displays the supernatural gloom over Egypt. The verse displays the Moses: stretching. The verse displays the Egypt: in darkness. The verse displays the judgment: fulfilled. The three days' thick darkness displays the ninth plague: a thick darkness three days (v.21) — the supernatural, oppressive gloom over all Egypt. The verse displays the distinction of Israel's light (v.23). That this thick darkness fell upon the land which worshipped the sun as its chief god shows the LORD executing judgment upon the gods of Egypt (Exodus 12:12), blotting out the light of the heavens that the proud nation adored, and shutting up its people in a gloom that no lamp of theirs could pierce (Exodus 10:23).
23:
"They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days: but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings." The witnesses note the darkness on Egypt while Israel had light — the crux of the distinction. On "They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days," the grammatical voices note the darkness. Ellicott and Gill note that "They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days" — the darkness was so thick that the Egyptians could not see one another, and none stirred from his place (rooted, paralyzed, in the darkness) for three days; the terror and helplessness (the people frozen in the dark, unable to move or work); the oppressive, supernatural gloom.

On "but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings," interpreters expound the distinction. The reformed and the dominant reading note the great contrast — "but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings": while Egypt lay in thick darkness, Israel (in Goshen) had light in their dwellings; the marked distinction (as with the flies, Exodus 8:22; the murrain, Exodus 9:4-6) between God's people and the world; the witnesses note the lesson — God distinguishes between his own and the world (Exodus 11:7; Malachi 3:18), giving light to his people in the day of the world's darkness; the type — light for God's people, darkness for the world (the children of light and the children of darkness, 1 Thessalonians 5:5; the light of God's countenance on his own while the world is in judgment, Isaiah 60:2, "darkness shall cover the earth... but the LORD shall arise upon thee"); the providence sheltering his own in the day of judgment.

On the distinction, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the mercy: "all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings" — the special mercy (Israel exempt from the darkness, having light); the distinction of grace; the providence (the LORD distinguishing his people, a token of the covenant, the light of the redeemed). The verse displays the darkness on Egypt while Israel had light.

The witnesses agree the substance: the Egyptians lay in thick darkness three days, unable to see or move, while Israel had light in their dwellings (Ellicott, Gill, Wesley). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the distinction: "all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings" — the marked difference between God's people and the world (Exodus 8:22; 11:7; Malachi 3:18), light for the redeemed in the day of the world's darkness; the type of the children of light and the children of darkness (1 Thessalonians 5:5; Isaiah 60:2), the providence sheltering his own in the day of judgment. The verse displays the light-and-darkness crux. The verse marks the darkness: on Egypt. The verse displays the light: in Israel's dwellings. The verse displays the distinction: of grace. The verse displays the type: light and darkness. The distinction displays God's light on his people in the world's darkness. The verse displays the Egypt: in darkness. The verse displays the Israel: in light. The verse displays the LORD: distinguishing. The darkness on Egypt while Israel had light displays the crux: the Egyptians in darkness, but Israel had light in their dwellings (Exodus 8:22; 11:7; 1 Thessalonians 5:5; Isaiah 60:2) — the distinction between God's people and the world, light for the redeemed in the day of judgment. The verse displays Pharaoh's fourth compromise (v.24).
24:
"And Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the LORD; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you." The witnesses note Pharaoh's fourth compromise — leave the cattle. On "Go ye, serve the LORD... let your little ones also go with you," the grammatical voices note the concession. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the LORD... let your little ones also go with you" — Pharaoh, under the darkness, called Moses and conceded more than before: now even the little ones might go (a step beyond the third compromise, v.11); the further yielding under the plague; the relenting growing under pressure.

On "only let your flocks and your herds be stayed," interpreters expound the fourth compromise. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the snare — "only let your flocks and your herds be stayed": Pharaoh's fourth compromise — let the people go, even the children, but keep back the flocks and herds (the cattle to be held in Egypt, as a pledge of return, or as plunder); the half-release (granting the people, but keeping their substance); the witnesses note the lesson — another of the world's (and Satan's) compromises: serve God, but leave your possessions (your property, your wealth) in the world; the snare of the partial surrender (keeping back something for the world; not all given to God); the partial obedience God's people must refuse (all to be given to God — the flocks for the sacrifices, and as their own; nothing left in Egypt, Exodus 10:26).

On the compromise, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the refusal to come: Moses would refuse this too (v.26, "not an hoof left behind") — the full surrender required (all the people and all their substance given to God); the lesson against keeping back any part for the world. The verse displays Pharaoh's fourth compromise — leave the cattle.

The witnesses agree the substance: Pharaoh now let even the children go, but would keep the flocks and herds (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the fourth compromise: "only let your flocks and your herds be stayed" — the half-release (the people freed, but their substance held), another of the world's compromises: serve God, but leave your possessions in the world; the snare of keeping back something for the world; the partial surrender God's people must refuse, all to be given to God (Exodus 10:26, not a hoof left behind). The verse displays the fourth-compromise crux. The verse marks the concession: even the children. The verse displays the compromise: the cattle stayed. The verse displays the snare: substance held. The verse displays the surrender: partial. The compromise displays Pharaoh's half-release. The verse displays the Pharaoh: compromising. The verse displays the cattle: kept. The verse displays the surrender: incomplete. Pharaoh's fourth compromise displays the crux: go, but let your flocks be stayed (Exodus 10:26) — the half-release, serve God but leave your possessions in the world, the snare God's people must refuse, all given to God. The verse displays Moses' full refusal (vv.25-26).
25:
"And Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God." The very brief verse notes Moses requiring the cattle for sacrifice. The grammatical voices note the demand. Calvin and Gill note that "Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God" — Moses refused Pharaoh's compromise (v.24): the flocks and herds must go, for they were needed for the sacrifices and burnt offerings to the LORD; the demand for the cattle (essential to the worship); the refusal of the half-release (all the substance to be given to God).

On "Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings," the witnesses note the worship. Gill notes the need of the cattle for sacrifice (the burnt offerings to the LORD) — the flocks essential to the worship; the demand for all the substance.

On "that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God," the grammatical-historical note the purpose: "that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God" — the worship requiring the offerings (the cattle for the sacrifices); the full surrender (nothing left in Egypt); the refusal of the compromise (all given to God). The verse displays Moses requiring the cattle for sacrifice.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the demand — "Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice," the need (v.24, Calvin, Gill); "Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings," the worship (Gill); "that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God," the purpose (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the demanding Moses. The verse marks the demand: sacrifices and offerings. The verse displays the cattle: needed. The verse displays the worship: the sacrifice. The verse displays the surrender: full. The demand displays Moses requiring the cattle for worship. The verse displays the Moses: demanding. The verse displays the offerings: needed. The verse displays the compromise: refused. Moses requiring the cattle for sacrifice displays the full surrender: we must have sacrifices and burnt offerings to sacrifice to the LORD (v.24) — the cattle essential to the worship, the compromise refused. The verse displays the full refusal (v.26).
26:
"Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind; for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God; and we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither." The witnesses note Moses' total refusal — not a hoof left behind. On "Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind," the grammatical voices note the demand. Ellicott and Gill note that "Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind" — Moses demanded that all the cattle go, not a single hoof left in Egypt; the total refusal of the compromise (the complete surrender, nothing held back for the world); the famous phrase ("not an hoof left behind") — the whole substance given to God; the full obedience.

On "for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the worship. Calvin and Clarke note the reason (the cattle needed for the service of God) — the worship requiring the offerings; the full surrender (all to be available for God).

On "we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the readiness: "we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither" — Moses' wisdom (not knowing yet what offerings God would require, all the cattle must go, that they might have whatever God should appoint); the readiness for full obedience (the whole devoted to God, the offerings to be determined by God's command at the place of worship); the lesson — God's people give all to him, holding nothing back, ready to obey whatever he commands; the full consecration. The verse displays Moses' total refusal.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses demanded all the cattle go, not a hoof left, for the service of God, the offerings yet to be appointed (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the total refusal: "there shall not an hoof be left behind" — the complete surrender, nothing held back for the world; all the substance given to God (the flocks for the offerings, the full consecration); "we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither" — the readiness for full obedience, all devoted to God, the offerings to be determined by his command; the lesson that God's people hold nothing back, ready to obey whatever he appoints. The verse displays the not-a-hoof crux. The verse marks the demand: all the cattle. The verse displays the surrender: not a hoof left. The verse displays the worship: all for God. The verse displays the readiness: full obedience. The refusal displays the complete surrender to God. The verse displays the Moses: refusing. The verse displays the cattle: all to go. The verse displays the consecration: full. Moses' total refusal displays the crux: not a hoof left behind; all for the service of God (the full consecration) — the complete surrender, nothing held back for the world, the readiness to obey whatever God commands. The verse displays Pharaoh's wrath (vv.27-28).
27:
"But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh hardened again after the darkness. The grammatical voices note the hardening. Calvin and Gill note that "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go" — Pharaoh, after the darkness and the rejected compromise (v.24-26), was hardened again (the LORD hardening, the judicial giving-up, as v.20; Exodus 9:12) and refused to let Israel go; the renewed obstinacy; the broken concession; the hardening confirmed.

On "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the judgment. Calvin and Clarke note the LORD hardening (the judicial hardening of the obstinate, after the abuse of the mercies, Romans 9:18) — the divine giving-up; the obstinacy confirmed.

On "he would not let them go," the grammatical-historical note the refusal: "he would not let them go" — the renewed refusal; the broken concession; the providence (the obstinacy bringing the last and greatest plague, the death of the firstborn, ch. 11-12). The verse displays Pharaoh hardened again after the darkness.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the hardening — "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go," the renewed obstinacy (v.20, Calvin, Gill); "the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart," the judgment (Romans 9:18, Calvin, Clarke); "he would not let them go," the refusal (ch. 11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardened Pharaoh. The verse marks the hardening: by the LORD. The verse displays the concession: broken. The verse displays the refusal: renewed. The verse displays the plague: the last to come. The hardening displays Pharaoh's renewed obstinacy. The verse displays the LORD: hardening. The verse displays the Pharaoh: refusing. The verse displays the firstborn: the plague to come. Pharaoh hardened again after the darkness displays the renewed obstinacy: the LORD hardened his heart, he would not let them go (v.20; Romans 9:18) — the broken concession, the obstinacy bringing the last plague. The verse displays Pharaoh's threat (v.28). That the LORD hardened the heart of the king even after this further concession had been offered and refused shows the providence pressing on toward the last and greatest of the plagues, the death of the firstborn, by which alone the obstinate heart would at last be broken and Israel thrust out of the land (Exodus 11:1; 12:29-31).
28:
"And Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more; for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die." The witnesses note Pharaoh's threat to Moses. On "Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more," the grammatical voices note the wrath. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more" — Pharaoh, enraged, drove Moses from his presence, forbidding him to come again; the wrath of the obstinate king (the rupture final); the threat (no more audiences); the proud king's fury.

On "for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the threat. Calvin and Clarke note the threat of death ("in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die") — Pharaoh threatening to kill Moses if he came again; the obstinate fury; the king's defiance to the last.

On the threat, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the providence: Pharaoh's threat (the final rupture) — the providence (the last interview, the next message, ch. 11, to be the announcement of the death of the firstborn; the king who threatened Moses with death now to lose his own firstborn); the obstinacy bringing the final judgment. The verse displays Pharaoh's threat to Moses.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wrath — "Get thee from me... see my face no more... thou shalt die," the threat (Keil, Ellicott); "see my face no more," the rupture (Gill); "in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die," the threat of death (Calvin, Clarke); the providence (ch. 11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the threatening Pharaoh. The verse marks the wrath: get thee from me. The verse displays the threat: see my face no more. The verse displays the death: threatened. The verse displays the rupture: final. The threat displays Pharaoh's furious defiance. The verse displays the Pharaoh: threatening. The verse displays the Moses: driven out. The verse displays the firstborn: the plague to come. Pharaoh's threat to Moses displays the obstinate fury: see my face no more, or thou shalt die (ch. 11) — the final rupture, the king who threatened Moses now to lose his own firstborn. The verse displays Moses' answer (v.29).
29:
"And Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more." The witnesses note Moses' answer that he would see Pharaoh's face no more. On "Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more," the grammatical voices note the answer. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more" — Moses agreed with Pharaoh's word: he would indeed see the king's face no more (in this manner, as a suppliant; the negotiations ended); the solemn finality (the last interview before the death of the firstborn); the prophet's dignified answer.

On "Thou hast spoken well," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the agreement. Wesley and Clarke note Moses' agreement (taking Pharaoh at his word) — the end of the pleadings; the solemn close (the king's obstinacy having sealed his doom).

On "I will see thy face again no more," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the finality: "I will see thy face again no more" — the last interview (Moses would not come again to plead; the next would be the night of the death of the firstborn, when Pharaoh would summon him, Exodus 12:31, yet, the witnesses harmonize, Moses delivered the final word, ch. 11:4-8, before fully departing, or saw him no more as a petitioner); the solemn finality; the doom sealed (the obstinate king given over to the last plague). The verse displays Moses' answer that he would see Pharaoh's face no more.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more," the finality (Ellicott, Gill); "Thou hast spoken well," the agreement (Wesley, Clarke); "I will see thy face again no more," the solemn close (Exodus 12:31, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering Moses. The verse marks the answer: spoken well. The verse displays the face: no more. The verse displays the finality: solemn. The verse displays the doom: sealed. The answer displays Moses' solemn close of the pleadings. The verse displays the Moses: answering. The verse displays the negotiations: ended. The verse displays the firstborn: the plague to come. Moses' answer that he would see Pharaoh's face no more displays the solemn finality: thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face no more (Exodus 12:31) — the last interview, the pleadings ended, the king's doom sealed. The verse closes the chapter, before the death of the firstborn announced (ch.11).
